sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Adrenal insufficiency can deteriorate into adrenal crisis with shock, hypoglycaemia, electrolyte disturbance, vomiting, and altered conscious state. Prehospital priorities are early recognition, low-threshold escalation in patients with known adrenal disease or recent long-term steroid exposure, treatment of reversible threats, protocol-led steroid replacement where available, and urgent transport.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Known adrenal insufficiency, adrenal action plan, or current/recent long-term glucocorticoid therapy with acute deterioration
- Weakness, lethargy, dizziness, abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhoea, or severe undifferentiated pain
- Hypotension, postural collapse, shock, or syncope
- Hypoglycaemia, confusion, reduced conscious state, or seizure
- Intercurrent illness, trauma, childbirth, or major psychological stress as common precipitants

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Sepsis, dehydration, hypoglycaemia, and hyperkalaemia may coexist and may all contribute to shock
- A patient or carer may have emergency hydrocortisone and a written adrenal insufficiency action plan
- Diagnosis can be challenging and may progress insidiously; do not delay treatment while waiting for diagnostic certainty in a high-risk patient
- Consider adrenal crisis in unexplained collapse or refractory hypotension, especially in patients with known endocrine disease or current/recent long-term steroid exposure

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Check BGL early and treat hypoglycaemia promptly within scope
- Give oxygen only if clinically indicated
- Keep the patient warm and perform frequent reassessment of blood pressure, conscious state, respiratory effort, and perfusion
- Obtain medication history, trigger history, and any patient-held adrenal action plan if this does not delay care
- Prioritise urgent transport for shock, reduced conscious state, recurrent vomiting, severe weakness, or suspected electrolyte disturbance

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- ECG
- Gain IV access early if adrenal crisis is suspected
- **Administer hydrocortisone** within local protocol, scope, or senior direction - hydrocortisone is life-saving in adrenal crisis; do not unreasonably delay administration
  - IM and IV routes are equally effective for hydrocortisone
  - Some patients carry their own emergency hydrocortisone kit (e.g., Solu-Cortef) - use it if available and within protocol, with senior consultation
  - **Ultra-rapid metabolisers:** A subset of patients with known adrenal insufficiency require 3× the standard hydrocortisone dose due to accelerated drug metabolism - check the patient's action plan
- Provide isotonic crystalloid therapy for hypotension, shock, or dehydration, with repeated reassessment
- Treat hypoglycaemia with oral, IV, or IM therapy according to the patient's condition and local protocol
- Monitor for hyperkalaemia (ECG changes) and hyponatraemia
- Escalate early - hypotension, reduced conscious state, recurrent vomiting, or severe weakness require Priority 1 transport with pre-notification

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Known adrenal-insufficient patients may deteriorate rapidly during illness, trauma, prolonged vomiting, or sudden steroid interruption
- Short-acting glucocorticoids have a strong safety profile and failure to treat true adrenal crisis is more dangerous than timely protocol-led replacement
- Record baseline steroid therapy if known, trigger history, BGL trend, blood pressure response, and whether emergency hydrocortisone was available or given
